source.title: Dopamine builds and reveals reward-associated latent behavioral attractors

confidence_rationale: Strong direct evidence that recurrent networks develop stable latent attractors encoding reward-associated goals through dopamine-dependent plasticity. The MAGNet model is close in spirit to REE's z_goal sub-space: an attractor in behavioral/latent state space that is locally accessible after training and globally accessible under motivational neuromodulation. Transfer risk is moderate because (1) the model encodes approach-only goals, not an explicit separation from harm, and (2) the biological substrate is striatal/mesolimbic rather than a hippocampal terrain map.
failure_signatures: If z_goal fails to develop a stable positive attractor even when SD-012 drive is active, this paper's mechanism predicts the failure lies in the absence of a motivational neuromodulatory signal equivalent to dopamine excitability widening.

If z_goal norm stays near zero despite correct homeostatic drive, the attractor basin may not be wide enough -- analogous to latent attractors that are locally stable but globally inaccessible without neuromodulation.

mapping.source_claim_statement: Dopamine-dependent synaptic plasticity creates latent attractors encoding reward goals in a recurrent network; dopamine neuromodulation widens attractor basins, making goals globally accessible from any state. Validated optogenetically in mice.
mapping.ree_translation: REE MECH-230 posits z_goal as a structured positive attractor in E3's latent sub-space, distinct from harm avoidance, seeded by homeostatic drive (SD-012). The MAGNet latent attractor maps onto z_goal's role: a persistent goal state that navigation (hippocampal terrain traversal) is oriented toward. The motivational dopamine signal in MAGNet parallels SD-012's drive_level scaling the z_goal seed.
mapping.mapping_caveat: MAGNet models a scalar behavioral attractor in a single-goal navigational task; it does not demonstrate separation of goal from harm in the same latent space. MECH-230 specifically requires that z_goal and z_harm occupy distinct sub-spaces -- this paper supports the goal-attractor half of that claim but does not directly address the separation from avoidance.
mapping.source_context: Recurrent network model (MAGNet) + dopamine optogenetic conditioning in mice; navigational approach tasks; no explicit harm/avoidance condition.

confidence_components.notes: High source quality (Nature Communications, peer-reviewed, in vivo validation). Mapping fidelity good for the attractor-formation half of MECH-230 but incomplete for approach/harm separation. Transfer risk moderate: biological recurrent network to REE's discrete latent sub-space, and biological dopamine to SD-012 drive scalar.
